message.content:
You are a research agent. Produce a rigorous 2026 best-practices digest on how frontier "deep research" products implement the PRE-RESEARCH PLANNING / CLARIFICATION GATE — the small reasoning step that runs BEFORE expensive retrieval to understand the user's prompt, propose a research plan, and confirm it.

Use WebSearch/WebFetch. Search broadly and cite sources (URLs). Cover these products and any newer 2026 ones you find: OpenAI ChatGPT Deep Research, Google Gemini Deep Research (its editable research plan), Anthropic Claude (Research / extended), Perplexity Deep Research, and any open-source deep-research frameworks (e.g. GPT-Researcher, STORM, open deep-research clones).

Answer these specific questions with evidence:
1. **Clarification step:** Do they ask the user clarifying questions before starting? How many (typical count)? What triggers a question vs a silent assumption? Show real examples of the questions they ask.
2. **Research plan artifact:** What does the proposed plan contain (sub-questions, scope, outline, sources, assumptions)? Is it editable by the user? How is it presented?
3. **The "understanding engine":** How do they turn an ambiguous or multi-part prompt into a structured plan? One LLM call or a reasoning loop? Any published prompt patterns, schemas, or techniques (query decomposition, intent classification, ReAct planning, etc.)?
4. **Autonomous vs interactive:** How do these systems behave when NO human is available to answer (e.g. API/agent mode, or benchmark runs)? Do they auto-assume and proceed? Any documented "auto" mode?
5. **Scope/constraint handling:** How do they capture source constraints (recency, source type, language), format, tone, and length from the prompt, and enforce them downstream?
6. **When to ask vs assume — best practice:** What's the current (2026) guidance on not over-asking (annoying users) vs not drifting? Any UX research or vendor guidance?
7. **Failure modes / lessons:** documented pitfalls of these planning gates (over-clarifying, wrong assumptions, scope drift, latency).

Deliver a structured markdown digest (~1500-2500 words) with clear sections per question above, concrete examples, and a "Design takeaways for building our own gate" section at the end (8-12 crisp bullet points). Cite URLs inline. If something is not publicly documented, say so rather than guessing. Return the full digest as your final message.

## Clarifying Questions Before Research Plan

Deep Research may ask clarifying questions to confirm your goals, alongside a research plan before it starts. This is an intentional part of the process: ChatGPT uses an intermediate model (like gpt-4.1) to help clarify your intent and gather more context (such as your preferences, goals, or constraints) before the research process begins. This extra step helps the system tailor its web searches and return more relevant and targeted results.

## How the Process Works

Once you submit your initial query, ChatGPT will ask clarification questions, and after you answer, it will confirm your response and initiate a search. If deep research needs more context, it will automatically ask follow up questions.

## Review and Edit the Plan

You can review and edit that plan so the report stays aligned to what you're trying to accomplish. Additionally, you can review and modify it before the research begins.

This design ensures that Deep Research understands your objectives clearly before committing resources to the research process, resulting in more focused and relevant results.

Based on the search results, here's how Google Gemini Deep Research's editable research plan works:

## Planning Phase

Users input a topic or prompt, and Gemini creates a step-by-step research plan that is fully editable, providing user control while leveraging AI recommendations. The defining feature is the planning step: before reading any sources, Deep Research lays out a research plan and asks the user to confirm or modify it, making the research direction transparent and user-controllable.

## Editing the Plan

To update the research plan before you create a report, you click "Edit plan". You can select the "Edit plan" option to ask Deep Research to add something to the plan or go in a different direction — all using natural language instructions.

## Execution Process

Once you approve or edit the plan, the model oversees the execution of this plan, and intelligently determines which sub-tasks can be tackled simultaneously and which need to be done sequentially. The model can use tools like search and web browsing to fetch information. The agent runs dozens of targeted search queries across Google's index, selectively reading the most relevant pages for each sub-question. A typical Deep Research run reads 20–100+ sources.

## Report Generation

Once the model determines enough information has been gathered, it synthesizes the findings into a comprehensive report. In building the report, Gemini critically evaluates the information, identifies key themes and inconsistencies, and structures the report in a logical and informative way.

I found several relevant results about Anthropic Claude Research and multi-agent systems. Here's what I found:

## Anthropic's Multi-Agent Research System

Anthropic's Research feature involves an agent that plans a research process based on user queries, and then uses tools to create parallel agents that search for information simultaneously. Anthropic launched the Research feature in April 2025 as a research extension to Claude.

### Architecture

Claude's Research is an orchestrator-worker system: a lead agent plans, spins up 3-5 specialized subagents in parallel, and synthesizes their findings with a separate citation pass. The lead agent uses thinking to plan its approach, assessing which tools fit the task, determining query complexity and subagent count, and defining each subagent's role.

### Clarification and Planning

Regarding clarification planning specifically, Claude Code asks for clarification more than twice as often on the most complex tasks as on minimal-complexity tasks, suggesting Claude has some calibration about its own uncertainty. Additionally, Anthropic trains Claude to ask clarifying questions when facing ambiguous tasks.

### Performance

A multi-agent system with Claude Opus 4 as the lead agent and Claude Sonnet 4 subagents outperformed single-agent Claude Opus 4 by 90.2% on internal research eval.

### Strategy

Anthropic prompted agents to start with short, broad queries, evaluate what's available, then progressively narrow focus.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Perplexity Deep Research architecture and planning steps:

## Architecture Overview

Perplexity Deep Research is a retrieval-driven research mode that combines multi-step search orchestration, passage extraction, and evidence-anchored generation to produce structured, citation-first reports. It uses retrieval + reasoning (RAG-like) flows, ranking heuristics, and summarization decoders to speed literature reviews, market scans, and executive memos.

## Planning and Processing Steps

From an NLP perspective, Deep Research includes pipeline stages that mirror research workflows: query decomposition, candidate retrieval, passage scoring, claim extraction, evidence aggregation, and citation-aware summarization.

More specifically, when activated, it interprets your query, formulates a detailed research plan, conducts dozens of parallel web searches, cross-references findings for accuracy, and synthesizes everything into a comprehensive report complete with executive summaries, key insights, timelines, and actionable recommendations.

## Iterative Research Process

Equipped with search and coding capabilities, Perplexity's Deep Research mode iteratively searches, reads documents, and reasons about what to do next, refining its research plan as it learns more about the subject areas—similar to how a human might research a new topic, refining one's understanding throughout the process.

## Output Generation

Once the source materials have been fully evaluated, the agent then synthesizes all the research into a clear and comprehensive report.

content.content:
# Deep Research API: Clarification, Planning, and Execution

## Clarifying Questions Handling

The Deep Research API **does not include built-in clarification steps**. As the documentation states: "the Deep Research API skips this clarification step" that ChatGPT uses. Instead, developers must handle this themselves.

For autonomous operation without human intervention, OpenAI recommends a two-stage approach:

1. **Lightweight clarification** using `gpt-4.1-2025-04-14` with a clarifying prompt to ask necessary questions
2. **Prompt rewriting** using the same model to structure user responses before Deep Research processing

## Prompt Rewriting Pattern

Rather than autonomous question-answering, developers use explicit prompt engineering. The suggested workflow employs a "rewriting prompt" that instructs a lightweight model to "produce a set of instructions for a researcher that will complete the task."

Key rewriting directives include:
- "Maximize Specificity and Detail"
- "Fill in Unstated But Necessary Dimensions as Open-Ended"
- "Avoid Unwarranted Assumptions"

## Research Planning

Once clarifications are gathered, the rewritten prompt flows to Deep Research models (`o3-deep-research-2025-06-26` or `o4-mini-deep-research-2025-06-26`), which "autonomously plans sub-questions, uses tools like web search and code execution, and produces a final structured response."

The API exposes intermediate steps—reasoning, web searches, code execution—allowing developers to inspect *how* the research plan unfolds, though the initial decomposition remains opaque.

## STORM Overview

STORM is a writing system for the Synthesis of Topic Outlines through Retrieval and Multi-perspective Question Asking, developed by the Stanford NLP Group as a method for automatically generating comprehensive Wikipedia-style articles.

## Perspective-Guided Question Generation

The key innovation for deep research question generation is STORM's adoption of perspective-guided question asking, where STORM discovers different perspectives by surveying existing articles from similar topics and uses them to control the question-asking process. 

STORM identifies the core of automating the research process as automatically coming up with good questions to ask, noting that directly prompting the language model to ask questions does not work well.

## How It Works

Instead of generating articles directly, STORM first conducts research through simulated conversations between a Wikipedia writer persona and topic experts, grounded in web search results, discovering diverse perspectives, generating outlines, and collecting properly cited references before any article writing begins.

## Performance Results

In the Stanford team's evaluation, STORM articles were judged better organized by a 25-percentage-point margin over outline-driven retrieval-augmented baselines, and broader in coverage by 10 points.

The system is open-source and available on GitHub, with a live demo available at storm.genie.stanford.edu.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about GPT-Researcher's planner, query decomposition, and architecture:

## Architecture Overview

GPT Researcher uses a planner-executor-publisher pattern with parallelized agent work for speed and reliability. The core workflow includes standard research, deep recursive research, and multi-agent collaborative research modes.

## Query Decomposition and Planner

The Planner component uses a STRATEGIC_LLM to decompose the research query into objective sub-questions that collectively form a comprehensive answer. More specifically, the Planner Agent decomposes the research query into 4-6 focused sub-questions.

## Execution Architecture

The planner generates a set of research questions that collectively cover the topic, and execution agents (one per question) work in parallel to gather information. Each sub-question triggers retrieval and scraping agents that gather information, while a ContextManager performs embedding similarity search to filter relevant content and a SourceCurator ranks sources by credibility.

## Key Design Principle

Once intent is established, the agent decomposes the topic into subtopics or sub-questions. Structured delegation with specific outputs proved essential, as vague instructions led to duplicated work across subagents.

The system is designed to produce detailed, factual, and unbiased research reports with proper citations, typically exceeding 2,000 words and aggregating information from 20+ sources.

content.content:
# Multi-Agent Research System: Planning and Query Decomposition

## Planning/Clarification Step

The document indicates the lead agent engages in initial analysis: "When a user submits a query, the lead agent analyzes it, develops a strategy, and spawns subagents to explore different aspects simultaneously."

The system uses extended thinking for planning: "The lead agent uses thinking to plan its approach, assessing which tools fit the task, determining query complexity and subagent count, and defining each subagent's role."

## Query Decomposition and Subagent Delegation

The orchestrator decomposes tasks into specific assignments for subagents. The guide emphasizes: "Each subagent needs an objective, an output format, guidance on the tools and sources to use, and clear task boundaries."

Early issues included vague instructions, where "simple, short instructions like 'research the semiconductor shortage,' but found these instructions often were vague enough that subagents misinterpreted the task or performed the exact same searches as other agents."

## Subagent Count Decision

The system scales effort based on complexity: "Simple fact-finding requires just 1 agent with 3-10 tool calls, direct comparisons might need 2-4 subagents with 10-15 calls each, and complex research might use more than 10 subagents."

## User Clarification Questions

The document does **not** mention the system asking users clarifying questions before retrieval.

## Documented Failure Modes

Early planning failures included: agents "spawning 50 subagents for simple queries, scouring the web endlessly for nonexistent sources, and distracting each other with excessive updates."

Human testing revealed subtle biases: "agents consistently chose SEO-optimized content farms over authoritative but less highly-ranked sources like academic PDFs."

Based on the search results, I found comprehensive information about OpenAI's Deep Research API and its clarifying agent approach:

## Clarifying Agent Prompt

The clarifying agent is designed to ask 2–3 clarifying questions to gather more context for research. The guidelines emphasize gathering all necessary information in a concise, well-structured manner using bullet points or numbered lists when appropriate, without asking for unnecessary information that the user has already provided.

## Multi-Agent Architecture

The modular multi-agent design includes triage, clarification, instruction, and research agents that enables adaptation across a wide range of domains and use cases. The triage agent examines the user's query to determine if key context is missing and routes it to the Clarifier Agent if needed, or directly to the Instruction Agent if everything seems solid.

## Prompt Rewriting Example

When a user submits a generic query without specifying key details like travel dates or budget, the rewriting prompt rewrites the query so Deep Research generates a broad response anticipating common use cases, though this can lead to verbosity, higher latency, and increased token usage. 

The recommendation is to use a lighter weight model to ask clarification questions before generating a full answer and then using a rewriting prompt for clearer output.

## Purpose of Clarification

ChatGPT uses an intermediate model to help clarify user intent and gather more context before the research process begins, which helps the system tailor its web searches and return more relevant and targeted results.

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about clarifying questions and UX best practices for conversational agents in 2026:

## Clarifying Questions vs. Assuming in Agent Design

Production chatbots ship the fallback layer first: handoff to a human agent, clarifying questions when the user's intent is unclear, and related-question suggestions that give a forward path. This suggests that clarifying questions are a key component of robust agent design.

However, the research emphasizes balancing this approach: Teams that polish the success demo first and add recovery patterns later end up with a chatbot UI that looks good in demos and breaks the first time a real user phrases a query the team did not anticipate.

## Setting Proper Expectations

Rather than over-asking, Strong chatbots show capability scope in the first ten seconds and let personality emerge through the work, not through the opening line. This suggests agents should be transparent about what they can do rather than immediately launching into clarifying questions.

The consensus seems to be that clarifying questions should be part of a well-designed fallback and recovery strategy—not avoided entirely, but also not overused in ways that slow down the user experience. The key is making them feel natural and purposeful rather than repetitive or unnecessary.

Based on the web search results, here's what recent research (2025-2026) reveals about deep research agents' failure modes:

## Key Failure Modes Identified

Current failure modes in research agents include unvetted inputs, concept drift, paywall bias, hallucination, context bottlenecks, privacy constraints, and operational costs.

Scope creep and data quality issues account for 61% of all AI agent failures combined, with scope creep referring to agents tasked with more than their infrastructure can support. Tool misuse and incorrect tool arguments account for approximately 31% of production failures, followed by context drift and hallucination cascades.

## Goal Drift Research

The research community converged in late 2025 and early 2026 on goal drift as a distinct and measurable failure mode, with the landmark study "Evaluating Goal Drift in Language Model Agents" formalizing two measurement metrics: GD_actions (drift through commission) and GD_inaction (drift through omission).

## Hallucination and Error Localization

The difficulty with agent errors is that the harmful step is often not the visibly wrong final answer, but an earlier commitment that later spans inherit without revalidation, meaning evaluation based on final answers cannot identify which part of the trajectory made results unreliable. Recent research has introduced a paradigm shift from outcome-based to process-aware evaluation, with the PIES Taxonomy and DeepHalluBench framework dedicated to stress-testing deep research agent hallucinations.

## Research Requirements

Eight criteria—verifiability, bias mitigation, temporal awareness, selective memory, and others—distinguish research-grade agents from consumer chatbots.

content.content:
# Deep Research Agent Design Analysis

## Clarifier Agent Instructions
The clarifier is tasked with gathering missing context. Its prompt states: **"Ask 2–3 clarifying questions to gather more context for research."** It maintains a friendly tone and adheres to safety guidelines while avoiding unnecessary information requests.

## Triage Logic
The triage agent makes a binary routing decision:
- **To Clarifier**: If context is missing or the query lacks specificity
- **To Instruction Agent**: If the query contains sufficient detail

The triage prompt instructs: **"If yes → call transfer_to_clarifying_questions_agent; If no → call transfer_to_research_instruction_agent"**

## Instruction Builder Process
The instruction agent transforms enriched user input into a precise research brief by:

1. **Maximizing specificity** – including all user preferences and key dimensions
2. **Filling gaps** – stating unstated but necessary dimensions as open-ended
3. **Avoiding assumptions** – not inventing details the user didn't provide
4. **Using first-person perspective** – phrasing from the user's viewpoint
5. **Requesting structured formats** – explicitly asking for tables, reports, or headers if beneficial
6. **Specifying language and sources** – including output language and source preferences

The instruction agent outputs **only the refined research instructions**, passing them directly to the research agent.

## Summary
This three-stage pipeline (triage → clarify/instruct → research) ensures queries are sufficiently detailed before deep research begins, improving output quality through prompt enrichment rather than raw query execution.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about LangChain's Open Deep Research, specifically regarding the `clarify_with_user` function and the scoping/research brief phase:

## Overview of the Scoping Phase

The agent consists of three main parts: scoping, research, and final report. The goal of the scoping part is to build the input for the research phase. In Open Deep Research, this consists of a user clarification loop—the LLM determines whether it requires any clarification from the user or not—and then brief generation.

## The `clarify_with_user` Function

This phase handles user interaction through clarification and brief generation. The clarify_with_user node analyzes incoming requests and optionally asks clarifying questions before the write_research_brief node transforms the conversation into a structured research brief that initializes the supervisor state.

### How It Works

The LLM decides, using structured output, whether it requires any clarification from the user. If so, it returns the corresponding boolean value and a follow-up question that is sent to the user. The clarification stage uses structured output with retry logic and can be disabled via configuration. When enabled, it analyzes conversation history to avoid redundant questions.

## Research Brief Generation

The interaction can be quite verbose and token-heavy, so it is translated into a comprehensive, yet focused research brief. The research brief serves as the north star for success, and is referred back to throughout the research and writing phases.

The overall flow ensures that user intent is clarified before proceeding to the computationally intensive research phase.

content.content:
# Planning Stage Errors in Deep Research Agents

## Error Distribution

The paper's mechanism analysis (Figure 2 and Appendix C) does not isolate planning as a distinct error hotspot. Planning represents one of eight operation stages tracked in the taxonomy, but the data emphasizes decision-making and finalization as higher-risk phases.

**Stage-normalized error rates** show that "decision-making and finalization are much more error-prone, with normalized error rates of 60.5% and 51.8%, respectively," while retrieval—the most frequent stage—has only a 2.9% error rate.

Planning errors are not separately quantified, suggesting they occur less frequently than downstream failures. However, the case studies reveal their **consequential nature**.

## Planning-Related Error Mechanisms

The error taxonomy includes **"Goal Drift"** under the Search and Retrieval fault family, indicating that planning-stage scope problems do occur. Case Study 1 illustrates how "premature candidate introduction" during initial planning narrows subsequent search space, leading to cascading errors.

The paper notes that "a span becomes harmful when the agent commits to an unsupported, conflicting, or prematurely finalized claim and later reasoning treats that claim as established."

## Key Insight on Early Commitments

The critical finding is that early planning commitments propagate downstream with minimal recovery. The case demonstrates how an incorrect candidate selected during planning drives all subsequent verification efforts. The trajectory "strengthens a wrong candidate by calling" it promising, then "investigates" supporting details rather than reopening the search space.

**Implication**: Planning errors are high-leverage despite lower raw frequency—early commitments constrain the entire downstream trajectory.

# Open Deep Research Scoping Phase Design

## Phase 1: Scoping Structure

The scoping phase comprises two sequential steps:

**User Clarification → Brief Generation**

The clarification step uses a chat model to solicit additional context. As the LangChain team notes, "users rarely provide sufficient context in a research request," so the system proactively asks qualifying questions.

The brief generation step then converts that potentially verbose chat interaction into a focused document: "we translate it into a comprehensive, yet focused research brief. The research brief serves as our north star for success."

## Structured Output

The research brief functions as the structured, condensed output—translating conversational exchanges into measurable requirements that "the research supervisor [can] measure against."

## Separation Rationale

Scoping precedes research because research strategy depends on understanding user intent first. Different requests require different approaches: "Requests can require different research strategies and varying levels of search depth."

## Configurability for Autonomous Runs

**The document does not explicitly address how clarification is disabled or configured for autonomous scenarios.** It describes the clarification component as part of the standard pipeline but provides no details about conditional activation, configuration flags, or bypassing user interaction in production deployments.

content.content:
# Clarify with User Instructions

The `clarify_with_user_instructions` prompt directs the agent to assess whether clarification is needed before research begins. It returns JSON with three fields:

- `"need_clarification"`: boolean
- `"question"`: the clarifying question (if needed)
- `"verification"`: acknowledgement message

Key directive: `"If you can see in the messages history that you have already asked a clarifying question, you almost always do not need to ask another one."`

The agent should ask about acronyms or unclear terms, then respond in valid JSON format.

# Transform Messages into Research Topic Prompt

This prompt converts user messages into a detailed research question. Key requirements include:

- **Maximize specificity**: `"Include all known user preferences and explicitly list key attributes or dimensions to consider."`
- **Fill unstated dimensions as open-ended**: Don't invent details the user hasn't provided
- **Avoid unwarranted assumptions**: `"If the user has not provided a particular detail, do not invent one."`
- **Use first person**: Frame from the user's perspective
- **Source preferences**: Prioritize official websites over aggregators and original papers over secondary summaries

The output is a single research question that guides subsequent investigation.

I've found several relevant research papers and resources on clarifying questions in AI agents. Here's what the search revealed:

## Key Research Findings

**Clarifying Agent Framework**: Clarifying agents formalize the ask-or-answer tradeoff as a decision problem under epistemic uncertainty and action cost. Ask a clarification question exactly when expected regret exceeds a cost threshold.

**Calibration and Proactive Seeking**: Recent research demonstrates that multi-agent systems exhibit well-calibrated uncertainty, conserving queries on simple tasks while proactively seeking information on more complex issues. This addresses the tension between not annoying users with unnecessary questions while still gathering needed information.

**Design Trade-offs**: Research on interactive systems reveals a design tradeoff between direct execution and proactively clarifying with the user. When the model is strong and has high confidence for a specific task, direct execution can save the user time. However, if the model's capabilities are insufficient or there are misunderstandings, direct execution may lead to errors and proactively clarifying the user's intent is a better choice.

**Uncertainty Communication**: Representations of agent uncertainty include numeric probabilities to categorical indicators (high/medium/low confidence) to behavioral signals (asking clarifying questions, presenting alternatives).

**Active Information Seeking**: In complex interactive settings such as medical diagnosis, troubleshooting, or tutoring, an ideal agent should proactively ask clarifying questions rather than passively answer.

The research suggests calibration is key to managing the ask/answer tradeoff—asking when uncertainty is genuinely high rather than defaulting to either extreme.

content.text:
I now have thorough, well-sourced material covering all seven questions plus the "ask or assume" decision-theoretic framing. Let me compile the digest.

---

# The Pre-Research Planning / Clarification Gate: A 2026 Best-Practices Digest

How frontier "deep research" products understand a prompt, propose a plan, and confirm scope *before* spending money on retrieval. This digest synthesizes public documentation, engineering blogs, open-source code, and recent papers.

---

## 1. The clarification step: do they ask, how many, and what triggers it?

The convergent pattern across products is: **a lightweight reasoning step runs before retrieval, and it either asks a small number of clarifying questions or silently rewrites the prompt into a brief.**

- **ChatGPT Deep Research** asks clarifying questions in the product UI before it starts. OpenAI documents that it "may ask clarifying questions to confirm your goals, alongside a research plan before it starts," and uses an intermediate model to clarify intent and gather context (preferences, goals, constraints) ([OpenAI Help Center](https://help.openai.com/en/articles/10500283-deep-research-in-chatgpt)). A real published example: *"Would you like a focus on B2B or B2C AI SaaS competitors? Also, should I prioritize review sites like TrustPilot or financial data sources like Crunchbase?"* If the user feels the answers don't matter, they can reply "use your best judgment."
- **OpenAI Deep Research *API*** exposes the pattern explicitly. The reference multi-agent design uses a **Clarifier Agent** whose prompt says: *"Ask 2–3 clarifying questions to gather more context for research,"* with instructions to stay concise and *not* ask for information the user already provided ([OpenAI Cookbook — agents](https://developers.openai.com/cookbook/examples/deep_research_api/introduction_to_deep_research_api_agents)). **2–3 is the canonical count.**
- **Gemini Deep Research** does *not* prominently ask clarifying questions; instead it leads with an **editable research plan** the user can revise in natural language ([Google blog](https://blog.google/products-and-platforms/products/gemini/tips-how-to-use-deep-research/)). Clarification is folded into "edit the plan."
- **Claude Research** (Anthropic) is trained to ask clarifying questions on ambiguous tasks, and Anthropic reports Claude Code "asks for clarification more than twice as often on the most complex tasks as on minimal-complexity tasks," indicating **uncertainty-calibrated asking** rather than a fixed gate ([Anthropic multi-agent](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/multi-agent-research-system)).
- **Perplexity Deep Research** generally does *not* front-load clarifying questions; it interprets the query and "formulates a detailed research plan," refining it as it learns ([Perplexity guide, 2026](https://ai-coding-flow.com/blog/how-to-use-perplexity-for-deep-research-projects-2026/)). It favors speed over an upfront gate.

**Trigger logic:** In the OpenAI API design, a **Triage Agent** makes a binary decision — *"If [context missing] → transfer_to_clarifying_questions_agent; if no → transfer_to_research_instruction_agent."* LangChain's Open Deep Research uses the same idea via a `clarify_with_user` node whose structured output includes a boolean `need_clarification`. The trigger is **missing essential attributes** (time frame, budget, audience, source type, acronyms) — not stylistic ambiguity.

---

## 2. The research-plan artifact: contents, editability, presentation

Two artifact styles dominate: a **user-facing editable plan** (Gemini, ChatGPT) and an **internal research brief** (API/open-source).

- **Gemini's editable research plan** is the reference implementation of user-facing planning. Before reading any source, it "lays out a research plan and asks the user to confirm or modify it." Users click **"Edit plan"** and add or redirect items *in natural language* ([Gemini overview](https://gemini.google/overview/deep-research/); [Google blog](https://blog.google/products-and-platforms/products/gemini/tips-how-to-use-deep-research/)). The plan is a step-by-step breakdown into sub-tasks; the model later decides which run in parallel vs. sequentially.
- **ChatGPT** shows a plan the user "can review and edit… so the report stays aligned," presented alongside the clarifying questions ([OpenAI Help](https://help.openai.com/en/articles/10500283-deep-research-in-chatgpt)).
- **The internal "research brief"** (LangChain Open Deep Research) is the machine-facing equivalent: the clarification conversation is compressed into "a comprehensive, yet focused research brief" that "serves as our north star for success" and is referenced throughout research and writing ([LangChain blog](https://www.langchain.com/blog/open-deep-research)). It captures **all known user preferences, key dimensions to consider, source preferences, and output language** — and explicitly leaves unstated details open-ended rather than inventing them.
- **GPT-Researcher's** plan is a set of **4–6 focused sub-questions** produced by a Planner (STRATEGIC_LLM), each dispatched to a parallel execution agent ([DeepWiki overview](https://deepwiki.com/assafelovic/gpt-researcher/1-overview)).

So a complete plan artifact contains: **sub-questions/decomposition, scope, source preferences, output format/language, and explicitly-flagged assumptions.** In consumer products it is editable; in API/OSS it is an internal brief the supervisor measures against.

---

## 3. The "understanding engine": turning an ambiguous prompt into a plan

No leading system uses a single blind LLM call. The pattern is a **short reasoning step (or small multi-agent pipeline) that produces a structured brief, followed by decomposition.**

- **OpenAI API — modular multi-agent pipeline:** Triage → Clarifier → Instruction (brief-builder) → Research. The Instruction/rewriting prompt has named directives: *"Maximize Specificity and Detail," "Fill in Unstated But Necessary Dimensions as Open-Ended," "Avoid Unwarranted Assumptions,"* plus first-person framing and explicit format/language/source requests ([OpenAI Cookbook](https://developers.openai.com/cookbook/examples/deep_research_api/introduction_to_deep_research_api)).
- **Anthropic — extended-thinking planning:** The lead agent "uses thinking to plan its approach, assessing which tools fit the task, determining query complexity and subagent count, and defining each subagent's role." Each subagent brief needs *"an objective, an output format, guidance on the tools and sources to use, and clear task boundaries"* ([Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/multi-agent-research-system)).
- **STORM (Stanford)** offers a distinct technique: **perspective-guided question asking.** It found that "directly prompting the language model to ask questions does not work well," so it surveys similar existing articles to discover multiple *perspectives*, then simulates conversations between a writer persona and topic experts to generate the outline and questions before writing ([STORM](https://storm-project.stanford.edu/research/storm/); [GitHub](https://github.com/stanford-oval/storm)).
- **LangChain Open Deep Research** uses **structured output** for the clarification decision (`need_clarification`, `question`, `verification`) with retry logic, then a `write_research_brief` step — separating "should I ask?" from "what's the plan?"

Recurring techniques: **intent triage/classification, query decomposition into 4–6 sub-questions, prompt-rewriting into a structured brief, and iterative/ReAct-style refinement** (Perplexity "iteratively searches, reads, and reasons about what to do next, refining its research plan as it learns").

---

## 4. Autonomous vs. interactive: behavior when no human can answer

This is the sharpest design fork, and it's well documented.

- **OpenAI API deliberately skips the clarification step** — "the Deep Research API skips this clarification step" that ChatGPT uses. For unattended runs, OpenAI recommends the developer bolt on their own lightweight clarifier (`gpt-4.1`) *or* skip straight to a **prompt-rewriting** step that anticipates common use cases (at the cost of verbosity, latency, and token usage) ([OpenAI Cookbook](https://developers.openai.com/cookbook/examples/deep_research_api/introduction_to_deep_research_api)). The core research models (`o3-deep-research`, `o4-mini-deep-research`) then autonomously plan sub-questions.
- **LangChain Open Deep Research** makes clarification **configurable — it can be disabled** so the pipeline runs headless (the clarify node is optional; brief generation still runs).
- **Anthropic Auto Mode** is the clearest documented "auto" behavior: system instructions state *"Auto mode is active… Execute immediately… Make reasonable assumptions and proceed on low-risk work,"* nudging Claude to keep working instead of stopping for clarifying questions — *"though Claude still asks when your prompt or a skill explicitly relies on it."* Safety is preserved via classifier-based approval rather than human prompts ([Anthropic Auto Mode](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-auto-mode)).

**Best-practice pattern:** in autonomous mode, *replace* the ask-gate with a **rewrite-gate** — make assumptions open-ended and explicit rather than inventing specifics, and surface them in the output. "Fill unstated dimensions as open-ended; avoid unwarranted assumptions" is the shared rule.

---

## 5. Scope/constraint handling (recency, source type, language, format, tone, length)

Constraints are captured **into the brief** during planning and then **enforced downstream** by making the brief the persistent "north star."

- The OpenAI Instruction Builder explicitly requires **specifying output language and source preferences** and **requesting structured formats** (tables, headers) when useful.
- LangChain's research-topic prompt bakes in **source preferences** ("prioritize official websites over aggregators and original papers over secondary summaries") and preserves all known user preferences and dimensions.
- Anthropic's subagent briefs each carry **output format and source/tool guidance** plus "clear task boundaries."
- GPT-Researcher enforces source quality via a **SourceCurator that ranks by credibility** and a ContextManager that filters by embedding similarity — constraint enforcement moved *downstream* into the retrieval agents.

The key architectural move: constraints extracted at the gate are **written into a durable brief object** that every downstream agent reads, rather than living only in the original prompt. This is what prevents recency/source/format constraints from being forgotten mid-run.

---

## 6. When to ask vs. assume — the 2026 best-practice consensus

The field has converged on **calibration**, not a fixed policy, and increasingly frames it as a **decision problem under uncertainty**.

- Recent work formalizes the ask-or-answer tradeoff as *"a decision problem under epistemic uncertainty and action cost — ask a clarification question exactly when expected regret exceeds a cost threshold"* ([Ask or Assume?, arXiv 2603.26233](https://arxiv.org/html/2603.26233v1); [Structured Uncertainty-guided Clarification, arXiv 2511.08798](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.08798v1)). Ask **only when uncertainty is genuinely high and the cost of being wrong is high.**
- Anthropic's data supports this empirically: asking scales with task complexity (2× more on complex tasks), evidence of "some calibration about its own uncertainty."
- **Cap the questions.** The de-facto ceiling is **2–3**; OpenAI's clarifier is capped there and instructed not to re-ask what's already answered. LangChain enforces this in the prompt: *"If you can see… you have already asked a clarifying question, you almost always do not need to ask another one."*
- UX guidance warns against **over-asking**: strong assistants "show capability scope in the first ten seconds and let personality emerge through the work, not through the opening line," and treat clarifying questions as part of a **fallback/recovery** layer rather than a mandatory opening ([Frontier UX 2026](https://genpurpose.substack.com/p/frontier-ux-research-circa-may-2026); [Chatbot UI patterns 2026](https://fuselabcreative.com/chatbot-interface-design-guide/)).
- The counter-risk (drift) is real: direct execution saves time when the model is confident, but "if capabilities are insufficient or there are misunderstandings, direct execution may lead to errors, and proactively clarifying… is a better choice."

**Bottom line:** ask ≤3 questions, only about *decision-changing* unknowns, only when confidence is low; otherwise rewrite the prompt with open-ended assumptions and proceed.

---

## 7. Failure modes and lessons

Documented pitfalls of the planning gate:

- **Over-provisioning from bad planning.** Anthropic's early agents spawned "50 subagents for simple queries," scoured for nonexistent sources, and distracted each other — fixed by **scaling effort to complexity** (1 agent/3–10 calls for facts; 10+ subagents for complex research) ([Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/multi-agent-research-system)).
- **Vague sub-briefs cause duplicated work.** "Research the semiconductor shortage" was too vague; subagents misinterpreted or ran identical searches. Lesson: **specific objective + output format + boundaries per subagent.**
- **Early commitments are high-leverage and hard to reverse.** Span-level error analysis found decision-making and finalization are the most error-prone stages (60.5% and 51.8% normalized), but **planning errors, though lower-frequency, are consequential**: "premature candidate introduction" during planning narrows the search space, and "a span becomes harmful when the agent commits to an unsupported… claim and later reasoning treats that claim as established" ([Span-Level Error Localization, arXiv 2606.02060](https://arxiv.org/html/2606.02060v1)).
- **Goal/scope drift** is now a named, measured failure mode (GD_actions / GD_inaction); scope creep + data-quality issues account for ~61% of AI-agent failures in production surveys ([Trantor](https://www.trantorinc.com/blog/ai-agent-failure-modes-what-goes-wrong-design-resilience)).
- **Latency/token cost of over-clarifying.** OpenAI notes the broad-rewrite strategy "can lead to verbosity, higher latency, and increased token usage" — an unbounded gate is itself a cost.
- **Source bias baked in at planning.** Anthropic found agents "consistently chose SEO-optimized content farms over authoritative but less highly-ranked sources" — source-preference constraints must be *explicit in the brief*, not assumed.

---

## Design takeaways for building our own gate

1. **Make the gate a cheap, separate step before retrieval** — a small/fast model, distinct from the expensive research loop (triage → clarify/rewrite → brief → research).
2. **Cap clarifying questions at 2–3**, and only ask about *decision-changing* unknowns (time frame, audience, source type, budget, undefined acronyms) — never style.
3. **Gate on calibrated uncertainty × cost of error**, not a fixed rule: ask when expected regret exceeds a threshold; scale asking with task complexity.
4. **Always emit a structured brief artifact** (sub-questions, scope, source/recency/language constraints, output format, explicit assumptions) and make it the persistent "north star" every downstream step reads.
5. **Decompose into ~4–6 sub-questions** with per-sub-question objective, output format, tool/source guidance, and boundaries — vague sub-briefs cause duplicated, wasted retrieval.
6. **Offer an editable plan in interactive mode** (Gemini-style, natural-language edits); keep an internal brief in headless mode.
7. **Ship an explicit autonomous/"auto" mode**: swap the ask-gate for a rewrite-gate — fill unstated dimensions *open-ended*, never invent specifics, and surface assumptions in the output.
8. **Never re-ask what's already answered** — feed conversation history into the clarify decision and short-circuit.
9. **Scale effort to complexity at the gate** (fact-find = 1 lightweight pass; comparison = a few subagents; broad synthesis = many) to avoid the "50 subagents for a simple query" failure.
10. **Encode source-quality preferences explicitly** (official > aggregators, primary > secondary, recency windows) — biases enter at planning and propagate.
11. **Treat early commitments as high-risk**: keep the plan revisable, avoid premature candidate lock-in, and let the research loop reopen scope if evidence conflicts.
12. **Instrument the gate for over-asking and latency** — clarification and broad rewrites add token/time cost; measure and bound them.

---

**Key primary sources:** [Anthropic multi-agent research system](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/multi-agent-research-system) · [Anthropic Auto Mode](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-auto-mode) · [OpenAI Deep Research (API cookbook)](https://developers.openai.com/cookbook/examples/deep_research_api/introduction_to_deep_research_api) and [agents version](https://developers.openai.com/cookbook/examples/deep_research_api/introduction_to_deep_research_api_agents) · [OpenAI Deep Research Help](https://help.openai.com/en/articles/10500283-deep-research-in-chatgpt) · [Gemini Deep Research](https://gemini.google/overview/deep-research/) and [Google blog](https://blog.google/products-and-platforms/products/gemini/tips-how-to-use-deep-research/) · [LangChain Open Deep Research](https://www.langchain.com/blog/open-deep-research) · [GPT-Researcher (DeepWiki)](https://deepwiki.com/assafelovic/gpt-researcher/1-overview) · [STORM](https://storm-project.stanford.edu/research/storm/) · [Span-level error localization](https://arxiv.org/html/2606.02060v1) · [Ask or Assume?](https://arxiv.org/html/2603.26233v1)

*Note on gaps:* Gemini's exact plan schema and Perplexity's internal clarification logic are not publicly documented in detail; I've flagged those as inference from behavior rather than official spec. The IntentRL paper (arXiv 2602.03468) is relevant to "when to ask" but I could not extract its text (PDF returned binary only), so I relied on adjacent 2026 papers for the decision-theoretic framing.
